description: Sequential scan — works on any list regardless of ordering.

examples.label: Target present — unsorted array
examples.notes: Unsorted input: binary search would fail here. Linear search finds 12 at index 3.

examples.label: Target at index 0 (best case)
examples.notes: Best case: target is the first element, O(1) comparisons.

examples.label: Target at last position (worst case)
examples.notes: Worst case: target is last, requiring n comparisons.

examples.label: Target absent
examples.notes: Target not present: all n elements scanned before returning -1.

examples.label: Duplicates — first occurrence returned
examples.notes: 1 appears at indices 1 and 3. Linear search guarantees the FIRST index.

examples.label: Single element — found
examples.notes: One comparison; target found at index 0.

examples.label: Negative numbers
examples.notes: Negative values are handled correctly by equality comparison.

precondition: arr is any finite list; no ordering required.
postcondition: Returns first index i with arr[i]==target, or -1 if absent.
invariant: After k iterations, target has not been found in arr[0..k-1].
reference: Knuth, D.E. (1998). The Art of Computer Programming, Vol. 3, §6.1
